1: shîr mizmôr libnê-qōraḥ — a Song, a Psalm of the sons of Korah. The double title (shîr: song for singing + mizmôr: psalm for instrumental accompaniment) is unusual — both vocal and instrumental performance indicated. gādôl YHWH ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd — great is the LORD and greatly to be praised. Gādôl (great) + mĕhullāl (passive participle of hālal: the one who is greatly praised). The incomparability formula applied to Zion-worship. bĕʿîr ʾĕlōhênû har qodshô — in the city of our God, on his holy mountain. The praise is not abstract — it is geographically located: in the city, on the mountain.
2: yĕphēh nôph śĕmōaḥ kol-hāʾāreṣ — Mount Zion, beautiful in its height, the joy of all the earth. Nôph (elevation, height — the aesthetic quality of the mount's height, not merely its altitude). Śĕmōaḥ kol-hāʾāreṣ (joy of all the earth — the source of joy radiating to the whole world). har-ṣiyyôn yarkĕtê ṣāphôn — Mount Zion, on the heights of the north. Yarkĕtê ṣāphôn (far recesses/heights of the north) is the most theologically loaded phrase: ṣāphôn is also the name of Mount Zaphon (Jebel el-Aqra, north of Ugarit) — the mountain where Baal held his court in Canaanite mythology. The psalm maps YHWH's Zion onto the cosmic mountain of the gods, displacing Baal: what was true of the pagan divine mountain is now true, and more truly true, of Zion. qiryat melek rāb — the city of the great King. Melek rāb (great King — the title for the supreme sovereign over other kings; cf. Ps 47:2; Matt 5:35).
3: ʾĕlōhîm bĕʾarmmĕnôteyhā nôdāʿ lĕmiśgāb — God has shown himself to be a fortress within her citadels. ʾArmmĕnôt (citadels, palace-towers — the most heavily fortified buildings of an ancient city). Nôdāʿ lĕmiśgāb (niphal: he has made himself known as a fortress) — the divine self-disclosure as protective strength occurring within the city's own strongholds. The defensive architecture of Zion testifies to the divine presence that makes it impregnable.
4: kî-hinnēh hammĕlākhîm nôʿădû — for look — the kings assembled. Nôʿad (niphal of yāʿad: to meet by appointment, to assemble — a deliberate, planned coalition). The kings who appear to be Zion's existential threat. ʿābĕrû yaḥdāw — they came against her together. The united advance — maximum military threat. The next verse (v5) reveals that their united power dissolves on sight of Zion.
5: hēmmāh rāʾû kēn tāmāhû — they saw it and were bewildered. Tāmah (to be astonished, confounded — the stunned reaction when one sees something that defeats all expectation). nibhălû nibbĕhālû — they were struck with terror and fled. The intensified niphal (nibhălû) emphasizes the sudden panic. The alliteration in the Hebrew (nibhălû nibbĕhālû: they were terrified / they fled in terror) mimics the frantic flight. The military coalition collapses at the sight of the city — without the need for any described battle.
6: rĕʿādāh ʾăḥāzātam shām — trembling took hold of them there. Rĕʿādāh (trembling, shaking — the involuntary physical response to terror). Shām (there — in that very place where they came to conquer). ḥîl kĕyôledāh — pain like that of a woman giving birth. Ḥîl (writhing, labor-pain — the intense, overwhelming physical pain of childbirth; a standard OT image for the paralysis that overcomes the apparently powerful in the moment of divine judgment, cf. Isa 13:8; Jer 4:31; Mic 4:10). The warriors' trembling reduces them to the helplessness of a woman in labor — the height of military irony.
7: bĕrûaḥ qādîm tĕshabbēr ʾoniyyôt tarshîsh — with the east wind you shatter the ships of Tarshish. Rûaḥ qādîm (the east wind — the dry, powerful sirocco that blows in from the desert; used of the divine weapon in Exod 14:21, Ps 78:26, Ezek 27:26). Tarshîsh (Tarshish — possibly Tartessus in Spain or another distant trading port; the ships of Tarshish are the grandest ocean-going vessels of antiquity, symbol of commercial wealth and power). YHWH's east wind shatters even the greatest human vessels — the same power that routes armies (vv4-6) also commands the sea.
8: kaʾăsher shāmaʿnû kēn rāʾînû — as we have heard, so we have seen. The conjunction of heard tradition and personal experience — the community's historical memory (shāmaʿnû: we have heard, the passed-down testimony) confirmed in their own sight (rāʾînû: we have seen). bĕʿîr-YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt bĕʿîr ʾĕlōhênû — in the city of the LORD of hosts, in the city of our God. The double identification of the city (Lord of hosts, Our God) emphasizes its divine ownership. ʾĕlōhîm yĕkhônĕnehā ʿad-ʿôlām selāh — God establishes it forever. Kôn (piel: to establish firmly, make permanent) + ʿad-ʿôlām (forever) — the permanence of Zion as the divine guarantee against all the kings who assembled.
9: dimmînû ḥasdekā ʾĕlōhîm bĕqereb hêkhālekhā — we have meditated on your steadfast love, O God, within your temple. Dimmāh (piel: to picture, meditate on, liken — the root is related to dāmāh: likeness, image). In the temple the community meditates — pictures, ponders, contemplates — the divine ḥesed. The temple as the space for theological reflection on God's covenant love, not merely for ritual performance.
10: kĕshimĕkhā ʾĕlōhîm kēn tĕhillātekhā — as your name reaches, O God, so does your praise. The proportionality of name and praise: wherever the divine name reaches, there praise follows — the geographical spread of YHWH's reputation is matched by the geographical spread of worship. ʿal-qaṣwê-ʾāreṣ — to the ends of the earth. The universal scope: from Zion (v1) to the ends of the earth (v10) — the psalm moves outward. ṣedeq māleʾāh yĕmînekhā — your right hand is full of righteousness. The divine right hand (yāmîn) — the hand of action and power — full of ṣedeq (righteousness, justice). What the hand is full of is what it distributes.
11: yiśmaḥ har-ṣiyyôn tāgēlanāh bĕnôt yĕhûdāh — let Mount Zion rejoice; let the towns of Judah be glad. The call to rejoicing: the mountain itself (har-ṣiyyôn) and the daughter-villages of Judah (bĕnôt yĕhûdāh: daughters of Judah = the surrounding towns). lĕmaʿan mishpāṭêkhā — because of your righteous judgments. Mishpāṭ (judgment/decision — the righteous verdicts) as the reason for joy: the divine justice displayed in the defeat of the kings is cause for communal celebration.
12: sōbbû ṣiyyôn wĕhaqîphûhā — walk all around Zion, circle her. The liturgical procession around the city — a pilgrimage act of inspection and praise. siprû migdālêyhā — count her towers. Māgdāl (tower — the watchtowers and defensive towers of the city wall). The counting is not military inventory but liturgical attention — seeing the city's defenses as testimony to divine protection.
13: shîtû libbĕkhem lĕḥêlāh — mark her walls carefully. Shît lēb (to set the heart toward — to give careful attention to; the idiom for deliberate, attentive regard). Ḥêl (rampart, fortification wall — the outer defensive earthwork). passĕgû ʾarmmĕnôteyhā — tour her citadels. Pāsag (to survey, look over carefully — a rare verb; to pass between and inspect). lĕmaʿan tĕsappĕrû lĕdôr ʾaḥărôn — that you may tell the next generation. The purpose of the inspection: to remember what you have seen and transmit it to the next generation. The tour of Zion's defenses becomes a pedagogical act — what you mark and survey today you can narrate to your children.
14: kî zeh ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhênû ʿôlām wāʿed — for this God is our God forever and ever. The climactic confession — zeh (this one — demonstrative, pointing to the God whose power has just been celebrated) is ʾĕlōhênû (our God). The Zion-theology grounded in covenant identity. hûʾ yĕnahăgênû ʿalmût — he will guide us until the very end. Nāhag (to guide, lead — the shepherd's or caravan-guide's leading). ʿAlmût (the word is ambiguous: it can mean ʿad-mût = until death = forever; or ʿal-mût = over/beyond death = past the death-threshold). Most read it as 'forever' (unto death = all one's life), but the consonantal text admits a reading of 'beyond death' — a faint ray of post-mortem hope within the Zion psalm.
